title: Human Preference Data
concept: Alignment requires gathering datasets where humans rank multiple model responses, creating preference pairs (chosen vs rejected).

exercises.prompt: When aligning an AI to be helpful and safe, what is the most important thing to measure?
exercises.explain: To align AI with human values, we must gather feedback from humans on which model answers they prefer.

cards.title: Teaching AI to Choose
cards.body: How do we teach an AI to be helpful, polite, and safe? We can't just write a list of rigid rules. Instead, we show the AI pairs of its own answers and let humans vote on which one is better!

cards.title: What is Preference Data?
cards.body: Human preference data is a special dataset where people review different answers written by an AI for the same prompt. Humans rate or rank them, labeling one as the 'chosen' (better) answer and the other as the 'rejected' (worse) answer.

cards.title: The Dog Trainer
cards.body: Think of training an AI like teaching a dog to fetch. If you throw a ball and it brings back a stick, you say 'no'. If it brings back the ball, you give it a treat. By constantly comparing two behaviors and choosing the better one, the dog learns exactly what you prefer.

cards.title: A vs B in Action
cards.body:
Imagine an AI is asked: 'Write a polite apology letter.' 

• Response A: 'My bad, sorry lol.'
• Response B: 'Dear customer, we are sincerely sorry for the trouble...'

A human labeler tags Response B as 'Chosen' and Response A as 'Rejected'. This pair becomes preference data!

cards.title: It's Not Just About Grammar
cards.body: Many people think AIs are aligned by just checking if their grammar is correct. But grammar isn't enough! An AI can write a perfectly grammatical sentence that is still mean or unsafe. We need human preference data to teach it helpfulness and safety, not just vocabulary.

cards.title: Chosen vs Rejected
cards.body: Preference data compares two AI responses side-by-side to highlight the better choice.

exercises.prompt: What does a typical human preference dataset contain?

options.text: A list of math equations solved by computers
Pairs of AI responses ranked by humans as chosen or rejected
A collection of random images downloaded from the internet
A set of rules written in a coding manual

exercises.explain: Preference data relies on comparing multiple AI responses to the same prompt and having humans decide which is better. This guides the AI to behave more like we want it to.

exercises.prompt: To build preference data, we need to generate multiple different responses to the same prompt so a human can compare them.
exercises.explain: Yes, preference data requires a prompt and at least two different responses so that a human can compare them and choose the better one.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing label type used in alignment datasets:
exercises.sentence: When comparing two AI answers, the preferred one is labeled as the [blank] response.
exercises.explain: In preference datasets, the better answer is labeled 'chosen' and the weaker or less safe answer is labeled 'rejected'.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks represents gathering human preference data?

options.text: Writing a single response to a user question
Labeling one AI answer as 'chosen' and a different one as 'rejected'
Correcting spelling mistakes in a Wikipedia article

exercises.explain: Comparing the quality of two different responses to the same question is the core of preference learning. The other options are just single text generation or grammar checking.

exercises.prompt: If you were hired to build a dataset for AI alignment, which of these tasks would you assign to your human labelers?

options.text: Write a program to scrape websites automatically
Show humans two AI answers and ask them to select the safer, more helpful one
Use a math formula to calculate how many words are in a response
Let the AI random-guess answers until it gets tired

exercises.explain: Human preference data requires showing the model a prompt, giving it two potential answers, and having a human choose which one is better (chosen vs. rejected). This creates the essential feedback loop needed to align AI behavior.
